source_paper_title: Marginal Values of a Stochastic Game
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Attia_2025, title={Marginal Values of a Stochastic Game}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0297}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0297}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Attia, Luc and Oliu-Barton, Miquel and Saona, Raimundo}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={482–505} }
context:
Section 6.2 (Compact-continuous stochastic games), page 16 (in the provided 19-page text)

Determining necessary and sufficient conditions on \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) which ensure the convergence of the discounted values or the existence of the value is a problem. Bolte, Gaubert and Vigeral [5] provided sufficient conditions, namely that \(g\) and \(q\) are separable and definable. Without going into a precise definition of these two conditions, they hold in particular when the pay-off function \(g\) and the transition \(q\) are polynomials in the players’ actions. However, the case where \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) are semi-algebraic is still unsolved.
title: Characterize convergence of discounted values in compact-continuous zero-sum stochastic games
background_latex:
A two-player zero-sum stochastic game is specified by a finite state set \(K=\{1,\dots,n\}\), compact metric action sets \(I\) and \(J\), a bounded stage payoff (to Player 1) \(g:K\times I\times J\to \mathbb{R}\), and a transition kernel \(q:K\times I\times J\to \Delta(K)\), both continuous in \((i,j)\) (with \(\Delta(K)\) the simplex over \(K\)).

For a discount factor \(\lambda\in(0,1]\), the \(\lambda\)-discounted payoff starting from an initial state \(k\in K\) is
\[
\mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{m\ge 1} \lambda(1-\lambda)^{m-1} g(k_m,i_m,j_m)\Big],
\]
where \(k_1=k\) and \(k_{m+1}\sim q(k_m,i_m,j_m)\). A (behavioral) strategy maps each player’s observation history to a probability distribution over actions; in these perfect-monitoring games, both players observe the current state and past actions.

Let \(v^k_\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\) denote the value of the \(\lambda\)-discounted game starting from state \(k\) (existence follows from standard minimax arguments under compactness/continuity). The family \((v^k_\lambda)_{\lambda\downarrow 0}\) is said to converge if \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists for each \(k\in K\).

One is also interested in the (uniform) long-run value \(v^k\), defined via the ability of each player to guarantee, for all sufficiently large horizons \(M\), an expected average payoff over the first \(M\) stages within \(\varepsilon\) of \(v^k\). In the finite-action case, this value exists and equals \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\), but in compact-continuous games the discounted values may fail to converge (there are counterexamples).

Known sufficient conditions for convergence include certain regularity/tameness assumptions such as \(g\) and \(q\) being separable and definable (in an o-minimal structure), which in particular holds when \(g\) and \(q\) are polynomial functions of the actions. A set \(E\subset\mathbb{R}^d\) is semi-algebraic if it can be described by finitely many polynomial inequalities; a function is semi-algebraic if its graph is semi-algebraic.
problem_definition_latex:
Give necessary and sufficient conditions on the primitives \(I,J,g,q\) of a compact-continuous two-player zero-sum stochastic game with finite state set \(K\) such that, for every initial state \(k\in K\), the limit \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists (equivalently: characterize when the discounted values converge as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\)), and/or such that the long-run value \(v^k\) exists.

In particular, determine whether convergence (or existence of \(v^k\)) holds when \(I\) and \(J\) are semi-algebraic sets and \(g\) and \(q\) are semi-algebraic functions.

location_in_paper: Section 6.2 (Compact-continuous stochastic games), page 16 (in the provided 19-page text)
open_problem_quote: Determining necessary and sufficient conditions on \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) which ensure the convergence of the discounted values or the existence of the value is a problem. Bolte, Gaubert and Vigeral [5] provided sufficient conditions, namely that \(g\) and \(q\) are separable and definable. Without going into a precise definition of these two conditions, they hold in particular when the pay-off function \(g\) and the transition \(q\) are polynomials in the players’ actions. However, the case where \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) are semi-algebraic is still unsolved.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: To refute proposed sufficient conditions, one can search over parameterized families of compact-continuous games and numerically test convergence of \(v^k_\lambda\) as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\), but the space of possible \(g\) and \(q\) is infinite-dimensional and convergence detection is delicate (requires resolving values accurately for small \(\lambda\)).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate conditions (e.g., structural restrictions on \(g\) and \(q\)) and candidate counterexamples can be represented programmatically within restricted parametric families (polynomial/semi-algebraic templates), with a fitness signal based on numerical evidence of convergence/nonconvergence of \(v^k_\lambda\) computed via discretization/LP approximations. However, the proxy may be noisy and computationally expensive for very small \(\lambda\), and proving necessity/sufficiency would still require theory beyond search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The compact-continuous finite-state zero-sum stochastic game model retains Shapley’s discounted value and stationary optimality for each fixed \(\lambda\), but the asymptotic regime \(\lambda\downarrow 0\) is delicate: Vigeral constructed a continuous compact-action counterexample where \(v^k_\lambda\) fails to converge, so no unconditional analogue of the finite-action Bewley–Kohlberg and Mertens–Neyman theorems can hold. Attia and Oliu-Barton’s PNAS paper clarifies where the finite-action convergence proof becomes unavailable in the compact-continuous setting: in the finite case, the auxiliary matrix-game value \(\lambda\mapsto \mathrm{val}\,W^k_\lambda(z)\) is eventually a rational function of \(\lambda\), enabling the limit \(F^k(z)=\lim_{\lambda\to 0} \mathrm{val}\,W^k_\lambda(z)/\lambda^n\) and hence convergence; this rationality breaks down with infinite action sets.

The main positive progress toward the open problem is the o-minimal/tameness approach of Bolte–Gaubert–Vigeral, who prove that if the payoff and transition are separable and definable (in an o-minimal structure), then the asymptotic value \(\lim_{\lambda\downarrow 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists. This covers many “tame” primitives, notably polynomial data (and more generally globally subanalytic/semialgebraic data in appropriate structures), and is currently the closest to a general criterion ensuring convergence in compact-continuous games. Beyond existence, Tauberian results for nonexpansive (Shapley) operators, such as Ziliotto’s, help relate discounted convergence to other evaluations (weighted averages, long-run averages) once suitable regularity is present.

However, a full characterization—necessary and sufficient conditions on \((I,J,g,q)\) guaranteeing convergence of \(v^k_\lambda\) (and/or existence of a uniform long-run value) in the compact-continuous setting—remains open. In particular, whether semialgebraicity of \(I,J,g,q\) alone suffices is not settled in the source paper’s account: definability plus separability is sufficient, but semialgebraicity without additional separability hypotheses is not known to imply convergence. Promising directions include identifying the precise “tameness” property needed to recover a finite-type description of \(\lambda\mapsto \mathrm{val}\,W^k_\lambda(z)\) near 0 (e.g., definable selection/stratification arguments), or constructing semialgebraic counterexamples to show additional hypotheses are necessary.

sources.title: Definable zero-sum stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives sufficient conditions ("separable" and "definable" in an o-minimal structure) ensuring existence of the asymptotic value \,\(\lim_{\lambda\downarrow 0} v^k_\lambda\)\, in compact-continuous zero-sum stochastic games; in particular covers polynomial (hence semi-algebraic/definable) data in many standard o-minimal structures.
sources.connection: Directly addresses convergence of discounted values in the compact-continuous setting by providing a broad tameness framework (o-minimal definability) under which convergence holds; it is the main positive result cited in the source paper for the open problem.

sources.title: A zero-sum stochastic game with compact action sets and no asymptotic value
sources.theorem_or_result: Constructs a compact-continuous two-player zero-sum stochastic game (finite state space, compact action sets, continuous payoff and transition) for which the discounted values \,\(v^k_\lambda\)\, do not converge as \,\(\lambda\downarrow 0\).
sources.connection: Provides the canonical obstruction showing that, without additional structure, convergence can fail; motivates the need for necessary and sufficient conditions and frames the open problem.

sources.title: A formula for the value of a stochastic game
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides an uncoupled characterization of discounted values (via parameterized matrix games \,\(W^k_\lambda(z)\)) and a tractable formula for the value in the finite-action case; explains why the value formula fails to extend to compact-continuous games and explicitly states the open problem of characterizing convergence in that setting.
sources.connection: This is the source paper framing the open problem; it isolates the technical point that breaks in the compact-continuous case (loss of rational dependence on \,\(\lambda\)) and points to definability as a sufficient condition.

sources.title: Stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves existence of the uniform value \,\(v^k\)\, for finite-state, finite-action zero-sum stochastic games and shows \,\(v^k=\lim_{\lambda\downarrow 0} v^k_\lambda\).
sources.connection: Serves as the benchmark positive result in the finite-action case; the open problem asks for analogues/conditions in the compact-continuous case where this theorem no longer applies.

sources.title: The asymptotic theory of stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows convergence of discounted values in finite-action stochastic games as \,\(\lambda\downarrow 0\), using real algebraic geometry (Tarski-Seidenberg elimination).
sources.connection: Highlights that algebraic-geometry tameness yields convergence in the finite-action case; conceptually related to the semi-algebraic/o-minimal approach sought in the compact-continuous setting.

sources.title: A Tauberian theorem for nonexpansive operators and applications to zero-sum stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops Tauberian-type results for nonexpansive operators (including Shapley operators) linking asymptotics of discounted and long-run/weighted evaluations under regularity of the evaluation weights.
sources.connection: Provides functional-analytic tools to transfer convergence between evaluation schemes once an asymptotic value exists; relevant for understanding long-run value and robustness, though it does not give a full characterization in the compact-continuous case.
